strategic_overview: The problem is best viewed as a positive-definite M-matrix/tree-lattice question with one 'bad' row: after sign reversal it is exactly the one-bad-vertex condition for negative-definite plumbing graphs used by Ozsváth-Szabó and Némethi. The standard graph-lattice theorem says that in a reduced graph Laplacian lattice irreducible elements are connected cuts; see Greene, The Lens Space Realization Problem, Proposition 3.3 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1010.6257), McCoy, Alternating knots with unknotting number one, Lemma 2.2 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1312.1278), and the thesis Alternating surgeries, Lemmas 4.21-4.23 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.05564). But the present lattice is not quite a graph lattice: adding v_infty = -sum of the vertices gives a superbase with all off-diagonal pairings nonpositive except precisely at the bad vertex, where v_infty · b = d(b)-w(b)>0. That single acute pairing is the whole difficulty.

directions.name: Rooted-branch Schur-complement attack at the bad vertex
directions.description: Assume first that 'a single vertex' means a unique bad vertex b. Root the tree at b. For each component C of T minus b, rooted at the neighbor r of b, let A_C be its positive-definite Gram matrix and let alpha_C = (A_C^{-1})_{rr}. Because every vertex in C is nonbad, try to prove recursively that alpha_C <= 1, with equality only in the extremal branch where all weights equal their original degrees. The Schur complement at b gives sum_C alpha_C < w(b), while the hypothesis gives w(b)<d(b). Then analyze a possible decomposition b = x + (b-x) by writing x = k b + sum_C x_C. A direct calculation gives x · (b-x) = k(1-k)w(b) + sum_C ((2k-1)c_C - x_C^2), where c_C is the coefficient of the root r in x_C. The aim is to prove a discrete branch inequality forcing this expression to be negative unless an extremal branch contains a norm-one vertex, in which case that vertex is immediately irreducible.
key_ideas: Use the bad vertex itself as the candidate irreducible vertex.
Positive definiteness is converted into Schur-complement inequalities on rooted branches.
If the bad vertex fails to be irreducible, the equality case should produce a norm-one leaf or a very rigid all-degree branch.
relevant_techniques: Némethi, Lattice cohomology of normal surface singularities, Section 2.2.3 effective versus nef cone sign control (https://arxiv.org/abs/0709.0841): inverse-matrix sign control for negative-definite plumbing matrices; after sign reversal this is exactly positivity of the inverse of the branch M-matrix.

Némethi, On the Ozsváth-Szabó invariant of negative definite plumbed 3-manifolds, Section 2.1 dual-basis sign observation and Lemma 7.4 Laufer algorithm (https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0310083): useful for proving the recursive alpha_C <= 1 estimate and characterizing equality.

BHM+16, The Prism Manifold Realization Problem, Proposition 3.2 and Proposition 3.5 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1612.04921): diagonal-weight monotonicity for decompositions of vertex or interval sums; increasing diagonal weights only makes nonnegative-pairing decompositions harder.

McCoy, Alternating knots with unknotting number one, norm-one obstruction implicit after Lemma 2.3 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1312.1278), and Alternating surgeries, Lemma 4.24 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.05564): a norm-one vector is automatically irreducible; in this tree setting a weight-one vertex gives an immediate solution.
directions.potential_obstacles: The decomposition coefficient k of b need not be 0 or 1; a complete proof needs a sharp integer branch inequality for all k, not just the easy k=0,1 cases. The real Schur-complement bound alpha_C <= 1 is probably too weak by itself; the equality and integrality cases must be controlled carefully.

directions.name: Near-obtuse superbase and graph-lattice comparison
directions.description: Adjoin v_infty = -sum_{u in V} u. Then {v_infty} union V is a superbase. For nonbad vertices u one has v_infty · u = d(u)-w(u) <= 0, while for the bad vertex b this pairing is positive. Thus the lattice has an 'almost obtuse' superbase with exactly one acute pair. Try either to mutate this superbase to an obtuse one, or to show that if every original vertex were reducible then the acute pair can be eliminated. Once an obtuse superbase is obtained, Proposition 3.4 of Gibbons identifies the lattice with a graph lattice, where irreducibility is governed by connected cuts.
key_ideas: The bad-vertex inequality has a clean superbase interpretation: it is exactly one positive off-diagonal pairing with v_infty.
If the acute pair can be removed or localized, graph-lattice irreducibility becomes available.
The nonbad part can often be realized as a graph lattice by taking b as a root and adding w(u)-d_T(u) root-edges at nonbad vertices.
relevant_techniques: Gibbons, Bounds on Alternating Surgery Slopes, Proposition 3.4 obtuse superbase gives a graph lattice (https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.0906): if the superbase is made obtuse, the problem becomes a connected-cut problem.

Gibbons, Bounds on Alternating Surgery Slopes, Lemma 3.5 mutation of an obtuse superbase along a -1 splitting (https://arxiv.org/abs/1412.0906): a model for local superbase surgery; here one needs an analogue for a single positive pairing.
Greene, The Lens Space Realization Problem, Proposition 3.3 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1010.6257): irreducibles in graph lattices are exactly ±[R] with R and its complement connected.

Alternating surgeries, graph-lattice realization by adjoining a root, Chapter 4.2 (https://arxiv.org/abs/1606.05564): if w(u) >= d_T(u), the vertex can be modeled by extra edges to a root; the bad vertex is precisely the obstruction to the naive realization.
directions.potential_obstacles: Known graph-lattice theorems require a genuinely obtuse superbase or an exact reduced Laplacian. The one positive pairing v_infty · b > 0 is not a small perturbation algebraically. Also, irreducibility of vertices in the nonbad principal sublattice need not automatically imply irreducibility in the full lattice after b is adjoined.

directions.name: Sign-reversed one-bad-vertex plumbing and Laufer sequences
directions.description: Reverse the sign of the form: set m(u)=-w(u) and change edge pairings to +1. The lattice becomes a negative-definite plumbing tree with exactly one bad vertex in the sense m(b)>-d(b). Delete or distinguish b; all other components satisfy the rational/no-bad inequalities. Use anti-nef minimal cycles and Laufer computation sequences to reduce arbitrary cycles to canonical representatives. The goal is to translate a reducibility witness u=a+b with a·b>=0 into a violation of anti-nef minimality or Artin positivity on a rational branch.
key_ideas: The problem's hypothesis is exactly the plumbing one-bad-vertex hypothesis after sign reversal.
Rational branches have very rigid Laufer sequences: added vertices have intersection exactly 1.
A reducibility decomposition should correspond to a cycle that can be improved by a vertex addition/subtraction; failure of improvement may identify an irreducible vertex.
relevant_techniques: Ozsváth-Szabó, On the Floer homology of plumbed three-manifolds, Definition 1.1 and Theorem 1.2/2.1 (https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0203265): exact sign convention for bad vertices and the one-bad-vertex framework.

Ozsváth-Szabó, On the Floer homology of plumbed three-manifolds, Proposition 3.2 full-path algorithm (https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0203265): finite vertex-firing normal forms for characteristic vectors; useful as a combinatorial template even without Floer theory.

Némethi, On the Ozsváth-Szabó invariant of negative definite plumbed 3-manifolds, Lemma 5.4 minimal anti-nef representative, Theorem 6.1, Lemma 7.4 generalized Laufer algorithm, and Definition 8.1 almost-rational graphs (https://arxiv.org/abs/math/0310083): directly adapted to the one-bad-vertex setting.

Némethi, Lattice cohomology of normal surface singularities, Proposition 6.1.4 generalized Laufer algorithm for nef reduction (https://arxiv.org/abs/0709.0841): clean coordinatewise-minimum proof of uniqueness and termination.
directions.potential_obstacles: The plumbing literature controls characteristic covectors, anti-nef cycles, and chi-sublevel sets, not irreducibility of the original vertex basis directly. A solver must build a precise dictionary from a decomposition u=a+b with a·b>=0 to a Laufer move or anti-nef minimality contradiction.

directions.name: Voronoi-relevant reformulation plus minimal counterexample
directions.description: Use the identity: a vertex u is irreducible in the problem's sense iff for every x not equal to 0 or u, one has u·x < x^2. This is exactly the Voronoi relevant-vector criterion. If no vertex is irreducible, then every vertex u has a witness x_u with u·x_u >= x_u^2. Choose such witnesses with minimal norm, support size, and then distance to the bad vertex. Use the tree's obtuse basis to show witness supports are connected and sign-coherent; disconnected or mixed-sign supports should split off with nonnegative cross-term, as in the D-type and graph-lattice arguments. The contradiction should come from summing or localizing these witnesses around the unique bad row.
key_ideas: Replace existential decompositions by a universal inequality against all lattice vectors.
Minimal witnesses should have connected support in the tree and very restricted signs.
Relevant vectors generate the lattice, so if no basis vector is relevant, the basis must be generated by non-basis relevant vectors with strong pairing constraints.
relevant_techniques: The irreducible vectors of a lattice: Some theory and applications, Theorem 1 Voronoi criterion and Proposition 3 relevant vectors generate (https://eprint.iacr.org/2021/1203.pdf): the original definition is exactly the relevant-vector inequality for u.

Greene, The Lens Space Realization Problem, Section 3.1 minimal-norm irreducible basis (https://arxiv.org/abs/1010.6257): every positive-definite lattice has irreducibles, but the task is to force one from the distinguished vertex basis.

BHM+16, The Prism Manifold Realization Problem, Proposition 3.9 sign separation and connected support for irreducibles in D-type lattices (https://arxiv.org/abs/1612.04921): sign and support arguments transfer well to any basis with nonpositive off-diagonal entries.

Bacher-de la Harpe-Nagnibeda style flow-lattice criterion as used in The Lattice of Integer Flows of a Regular Matroid, Proposition 12 (https://arxiv.org/abs/0908.4071): metric indecomposability detects circuit-like minimal support; useful as an analogy for converting metric irreducibility into support minimality.
directions.potential_obstacles: This direction may prove the existence of some irreducible or relevant vector, which is already known in broad generality, without proving that a vertex is irreducible. The hard step is forcing a relevant vector to coincide with one of the original basis vertices rather than with a connected subtree sum.

key_obstacles: The main barrier is that the strongest available theorems classify irreducibles in graph lattices, linear lattices, D-type lattices, or changemaker lattices, whereas the present tree lattice is only near those settings. Decompositions of a vertex u can involve arbitrary integer coefficients, not just a coordinate split into 0 and 1 parts, so the elegant diagonal support-splitting lemmas from changemaker lattices do not apply directly. Induction by deleting leaves is also delicate: a vertex irreducible in a principal sublattice may become reducible after the deleted leaf or the bad vertex is reintroduced. Finally, the phrase 'a single vertex' should be treated as meaning a unique bad vertex; if it only means 'at least one', several of the one-bad-vertex and almost-rational reductions may fail or need substantial modification.
recommended_starting_point: Start with the rooted-branch Schur-complement attack. The very first step should be to prove and record the exact formula for a decomposition of the bad vertex b: for x = k b + sum_C x_C, show x · (b-x) = k(1-k)w(b) + sum_C ((2k-1)c_C - x_C^2). In parallel prove the rooted nonbad-branch estimate alpha_C=(A_C^{-1})_{rr} <= 1 by induction from the leaves, using w(u) >= d_T(u) for all u != b. Then focus all effort on the integer equality cases of the branch inequality; if those cases force a weight-one vertex, the proof is essentially finished because norm-one vertices are irreducible.
